# Abstract

Diagnosis of interstitial lung disease (ILD) requires a multidisciplinary discussion approach that includes clinicians, radiologists, and pathologists. Surgical lung biopsy (SLB) is currently the recommended standard in obtaining pathologic specimens for patients with ILD requiring a tissue diagnosis. The increased diagnostic confidence and accuracy provided by microscopic pathology assessment of SLB specimens must be balanced with the associated risks in patients with ILD. This document was developed by the SLB Working Group of the Pulmonary Fibrosis Foundation, composed of a multidisciplinary group of ILD physicians, including pulmonologists, radiologists, pathologists, and thoracic surgeons. In this document, we present an up-to-date literature review of the indications, contraindications, risks, and alternatives to SLB in the diagnosis of fibrotic ILD; outline an integrated approach to the decision-making around SLB in the diagnosis of fibrotic ILD; and provide practical information to maximize the yield and safety of SLB.

# Keywords

interstitial lung disease; usual interstitial pneumonitis; idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis; surgical lung biopsy; diagnostic guidelines

FOCUSED REVIEW

Diagnosis of interstitial lung disease (ILD) hypersensitivity pneumonitis [HP], IPF vs. clinical context is uncertain. The guidelines requires a multidisciplinary discussion (MDD) approach that includes input from clinicians, radiologists, and pathologists (1). Pathology plays a key role in the diagnosis of patients with ILD. For patients requiring biopsy for diagnosis, surgical lung biopsy (SLB) is currently the recommended standard in obtaining pathologic specimens for patients with ILD (2). It is generally considered for patients who 1) lack a confident clinical–radiologic diagnosis after appropriate noninvasive evaluation, 2) are not at excessively increased risk for postoperative complications, and 3) are expected to benefit from a change in treatment on the basis of biopsy results (3, 4). Moreover, the pathologic pattern may inform the prognosis of patients with ILD (5, 6). The increased confidence and accuracy of the diagnosis gained from microscopic pathology assessment of SLB specimens must be balanced with the associated risks in patients with ILD.

The aims of this document are to 1) provide an up-to-date literature review of the indications, contraindications, risks, and alternatives to SLB in the diagnosis of fibrotic ILD; 2) outline an integrated approach to the decision-making around SLB in the diagnosis of fibrotic ILD; and 3) provide practical information to maximize the yield and safety of SLB. To achieve these aims, we convened a multidisciplinary group of ILD providers participating in the Pulmonary Fibrosis Foundation Care Center Network, which included 13 pulmonologists, 2 chest radiologists, 3 lung pathologists, and 2 thoracic surgeons with interest and expertise in ILD from 18 centers across the United States.

# Indications for SLB

Broadly speaking, SLB is indicated for fibrotic ILD when the results are expected to influence management decisions. The initial treatment approach to newly diagnosed ILDs generally follows one of three basic strategies: 1) antifibrotic medications currently approved for idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF) (and recently, nintedanib for scleroderma-associated ILD and progressive fibrosing ILD) (3, 4, 7–9), 2) immunosuppressive therapy (together with elimination of potentially harmful exposures), or 3) expectant management. SLB should be considered in cases in which the results are likely to shift diagnostic confidence sufficiently to select one of these three strategies (e.g., IPF vs. fibrotic).

# HP

The 2020 ATS/Japanese Respiratory Society/Asociacion Latinoamericana de Torax clinical practice guidelines for the diagnosis of HP categorize HP into nonfibrotic and fibrotic phenotypes on the basis of clinical, radiologic, and pathologic features (16). According to these guidelines, the diagnostic evaluation of possible HP should include 1) an exposure assessment that includes a thorough clinical history, serum IgG testing against potential eliciting antigens and/or specific inhalational challenge testing in centers with adequate experience; 2) HRCT evaluation that includes images being acquired in the supine position at deep inspiration and after prolonged expiration as well as review of images by a thoracic radiologist; and 3) bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) with lymphocyte analysis (16). A high-confidence diagnosis (80–89% confidence) of HP can be established if an exposure is identified, HRCT shows a typical HP pattern, BAL confirms lymphocytosis, and an MDD was performed. The typical HRCT pattern for fibrotic HP requires a pattern of lung fibrosis characterized by a random distribution both axially and craniocaudally, midlung zone–predominance, or relative sparing in the lower lung zones and at least one abnormality indicative of small airway disease, such as ill-defined, centrilobular nodules and/or ground-glass opacities or mosaic attenuation, a three-density pattern, and/or air-trapping (16).

It is generally considered for patients who 1) lack a confident clinical–radiologic diagnosis after appropriate noninvasive evaluation, 2) are not at excessively increased risk for postoperative complications, and 3) are expected to benefit from a change in treatment on the basis of biopsy results (3, 4). Moreover, the pathologic pattern may inform the prognosis of patients with ILD (5, 6). The increased confidence and accuracy of the diagnosis gained from microscopic pathology assessment of SLB specimens must be balanced with the associated risks in patients with ILD.

items.md: The aims of this document are to 1) provide an up-to-date literature review of the indications, contraindications, risks, and alternatives to SLB in the diagnosis of fibrotic ILD; 2) outline an integrated approach to the decision-making around SLB in the diagnosis of fibrotic ILD; and 3) provide practical information to maximize the yield and safety of SLB. To achieve these aims, we convened a multidisciplinary group of ILD providers participating in the Pulmonary Fibrosis Foundation Care Center Network, which included 13 pulmonologists, 2 chest radiologists, 3 lung pathologists, and 2 thoracic surgeons with interest and expertise in ILD from 18 centers across the United States.

items.md: # Indications for SLB
items.value: Indications for SLB

items.md: Broadly speaking, SLB is indicated for fibrotic ILD when the results are expected to influence management decisions. The initial treatment approach to newly diagnosed ILDs generally follows one of three basic strategies: 1) antifibrotic medications currently approved for idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF) (and recently, nintedanib for scleroderma-associated ILD and progressive fibrosing ILD) (3, 4, 7–9), 2) immunosuppressive therapy (together with elimination of potentially harmful exposures), or 3) expectant management. SLB should be considered in cases in which the results are likely to shift diagnostic confidence sufficiently to select one of these three strategies (e.g., IPF vs. fibrotic).

items.md: # HP
items.value: HP

items.md: The 2020 ATS/Japanese Respiratory Society/Asociacion Latinoamericana de Torax clinical practice guidelines for the diagnosis of HP categorize HP into nonfibrotic and fibrotic phenotypes on the basis of clinical, radiologic, and pathologic features (16). According to these guidelines, the diagnostic evaluation of possible HP should include 1) an exposure assessment that includes a thorough clinical history, serum IgG testing against potential eliciting antigens and/or specific inhalational challenge testing in centers with adequate experience; 2) HRCT evaluation that includes images being acquired in the supine position at deep inspiration and after prolonged expiration as well as review of images by a thoracic radiologist; and 3) bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) with lymphocyte analysis (16). A high-confidence diagnosis (80–89% confidence) of HP can be established if an exposure is identified, HRCT shows a typical HP pattern, BAL confirms lymphocytosis, and an MDD was performed. The typical HRCT pattern for fibrotic HP requires a pattern of lung fibrosis characterized by a random distribution both axially and craniocaudally, midlung zone–predominance, or relative sparing in the lower lung zones and at least one abnormality indicative of small airway disease, such as ill-defined, centrilobular nodules and/or ground-glass opacities or mosaic attenuation, a three-density pattern, and/or air-trapping (16).

Definition of abbreviations: ALAT = Asociacion Latinoamericana de Torax; ATS = American Thoracic Society; ERS = European Respiratory Society; HRCT = high-resolution computed tomography; IPF = idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis; JRS = Japanese Respiratory Society; SLB = surgical lung biopsy; UIP = usual interstitial pneumonia. Adapted by permission from References 1 and 10.

Reticulation is reported to have a high specificity (.90%) for HP (17). However, it is well known that fibrotic HP can have other HRCT patterns, including typical and probable UIP patterns (10, 17–20). Transbronchial cryobiopsy (TBCB) or SLB are considered in cases in which a confident clinical and radiologic diagnosis cannot be established after an MDD (16), although it should be noted that tissue diagnosis of HP can also be challenging. Although there are histopathologic features that are considered “typical” for HP, such as bronchiolocentric inflammation and/or fibrosis, poorly formed nonnecrotizing granulomas, and chronic interstitial pneumonia, similar features may be seen in the setting of infection or chronic aspiration (21). Conversely, a biopsy showing a predominant histologic pattern of nonspecific interstitial pneumonia or UIP may also be seen in the setting of clinical HP (22, 23). Lung biopsy findings should be considered in the context of clinical, serologic, and HRCT features at MDD.

For patients with suspected autoimmune disease who do not meet the criteria for a CTD, the use of SLB may be considered on a case-by-case basis. A framework for evaluating these patients has been proposed on the basis of criteria that include clinical, serologic, and morphologic (HRCT and/or SLB) “domains” under the nomenclature of interstitial pneumonia with autoimmune features (IPAF) (31). However, IPAF criteria are currently considered a research classification tool only.

For patients with a suspected drug-induced ILD, pneumoconiosis, or smoking-related ILD, a compatible exposure history and HRCT patterns may be sufficient to make a confident diagnosis.

# FOCUSED REVIEW

The diagnosis centers on the correct identification of the culprit exposure, temporal relationship, and characteristic clinical features and findings at imaging (32). Although, in general, there is no specific diagnostic histologic pattern for drug-induced ILD at SLB, a number of drugs have known patterns of histologic features (33–35). The microscopic pattern at SLB may provide complementary information to the clinical and radiologic information, would increase clinical confidence in the diagnosis, and may be considered on a case-by-case basis.

# Risks and Contraindications to SLB

There is no single absolute contraindication to SLB in the diagnosis of ILD. However, there are patient-level risk factors that are associated with increased morbidity and mortality after SLB in patients with ILD. For elective SLB, the risk of short-term (in-hospital) mortality from SLB has been estimated at 1.7% (36, 37), the risk of 30-day mortality has been estimated at 1.5–2.4% (37, 38), and the risk of 90-day mortality has been estimated at 2.8–3.9% (37, 38). For nonelective SLB procedures, short-term mortality may be as high as 16% (36), but some studies report lower 30-day (6.3%) (38) and 90-day (8.8%) (37–39) mortality rates. These risks depend on 1) the type of procedure (open lung surgery has higher mortality and more complications than video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery [VATS] [38]); 2) center-level factors (surgical expertise, anesthesia expertise, and SLB volume); and 3) patient-level factors such as medical comorbidities, older age, the physiologic severity of the ILD, the presence of rapidly progressive disease, the risk of short-term mortality from SLB has been estimated at 1.7% (36, 37), the risk of 30-day mortality has been estimated at 1.5–2.4% (37, 38), and the risk of 90-day mortality has been estimated at 2.8–3.9% (37, 38). # Alternatives to SLB

Although SLB remains the diagnostic test of choice for the majority of fibrotic ILDs, other forms of tissue sampling may play a role in the evaluation of patients with ILD. Invasive diagnostic procedures that are alternatives to SLB include BAL fluid analysis, transbronchial forceps biopsies (TBFBs), and TBCBs. In general, these methods provide less information for the diagnosis of fibrotic ILD than does SLB and carry their own specific procedural risks. The rate of major complications of BAL is <0.5%, and the rate of mortality is <0.05% (1, 44, 45). The procedure is relatively contraindicated in patients with cardiopulmonary instability or severe bleeding diathesis.

pages.md:
FOCUSED REVIEW

IPF guidelines report that for every 1,000 who require lung tissue sampling for an ILD diagnosis (56, 57), the evidence assessing the safety and utility of TBFBs performed, 780 are considered to yield adequate specimens. Of these, diagnoses are made in 360 cases, with the highest yield being found in ILDs with centrilobular disease (1). When performing TBFB, recent HRCT findings should be used to guide the selection of sampling sites (44). Ideally, at least four to six samples that are 2–3 mm in size should be acquired (44, 50, 51). TBFB should also be performed before the initiation of therapy when possible. The most common complications are pneumothorax (PTX), hypoxemia, bleeding, and arrhythmias. PTX occurs in approximately 10% of cases. Sampling under fluoroscopic guidance can reduce the risk of PTX. Bleeding is the primary limiting factor in obtaining larger samples. The risk of major bleeding is <1% (46). Contraindications include a known bleeding disorder, an international normalized ratio (INR) >1.5, a platelet count of <50,000/ml, and severe hypoxemia (46). Rarely, TBFB can cause an acute exacerbation of IPF (<1%) (46).

# TBCB

TBCB uses a bronchoscopic cryoprobe to apply a cooling agent, typically CO₂/nitric oxide under high pressure, which causes lung tissue to adhere to the probe. Subsequently, the cryoprobe with the tissue attached is withdrawn. The role of TBCB in ILD has been heavily debated because of some conflicting literature on its diagnostic accuracy, variable experience levels with this emerging technique, and occasionally ensuing complications (46, 52). When compared with TBFB, TBCB offers the advantage of providing larger tissue samples, a higher percentage of alveolar tissue, and fewer crush artifacts, leading to a greater diagnostic yield (52). Historically, there has been high heterogeneity in the reported diagnostic yields, which have ranged from 40% to 95.1% based on histopathologic tissue sampling alone (53, 54). The ambiguity surrounding the value of TBCB has led to several landmark trials over the last year, which will likely change the direction of future guidelines surrounding the use of TBCB. A study in 21 patients who underwent both SLB and TBCB in the same procedure demonstrated poor agreement between the diagnosis derived from TBCB findings and the diagnosis derived from SLB findings when the biopsy specimens were reviewed in a blinded manner and without MDD (55). However, more recent larger studies have demonstrated support for the use of TBCB as an alternative to SLB in patients.

# Integrated Approach to Decision-Making to Perform SLB

The overall diagnostic yield of SLB is close to 90% when considered in the context of MDD (1). However, SLB is not necessary to establish a confident diagnosis in most cases in which a confident clinical diagnosis can be established on the basis of noninvasive evaluation, as discussed above. Therefore, before consideration of SLB, a thorough evaluation of the ILD, including a detailed history and physical examination, HRCT, and serologic evaluation for autoimmune disease are critical (1, 10). By using the information obtained from the noninvasive evaluation, the following three questions may help to guide the decision whether to proceed to SLB:

1. What is the leading (or provisional) clinical diagnosis, and what is the level of confidence in this diagnosis?

Of these, diagnoses are made in 360 cases, with the highest yield being found in ILDs with centrilobular disease (1). When performing TBFB, recent HRCT findings should be used to guide the selection of sampling sites (44). Ideally, at least four to six samples that are 2–3 mm in size should be acquired (44, 50, 51). TBFB should also be performed before the initiation of therapy when possible. The most common complications are pneumothorax (PTX), hypoxemia, bleeding, and arrhythmias. PTX occurs in approximately 10% of cases. Sampling under fluoroscopic guidance can reduce the risk of PTX. Bleeding is the primary limiting factor in obtaining larger samples. The risk of major bleeding is <1% (46). Contraindications include a known bleeding disorder, an international normalized ratio (INR) >1.5, a platelet count of <50,000/ml, and severe hypoxemia (46). Rarely, TBFB can cause an acute exacerbation of IPF (<1%) (46).

items.md: # TBCB
items.value: TBCB

items.md: TBCB uses a bronchoscopic cryoprobe to apply a cooling agent, typically CO₂/nitric oxide under high pressure, which causes lung tissue to adhere to the probe. Subsequently, the cryoprobe with the tissue attached is withdrawn. The role of TBCB in ILD has been heavily debated because of some conflicting literature on its diagnostic accuracy, variable experience levels with this emerging technique, and occasionally ensuing complications (46, 52). When compared with TBFB, TBCB offers the advantage of providing larger tissue samples, a higher percentage of alveolar tissue, and fewer crush artifacts, leading to a greater diagnostic yield (52). Historically, there has been high heterogeneity in the reported diagnostic yields, which have ranged from 40% to 95.1% based on histopathologic tissue sampling alone (53, 54). The ambiguity surrounding the value of TBCB has led to several landmark trials over the last year, which will likely change the direction of future guidelines surrounding the use of TBCB. A study in 21 patients who underwent both SLB and TBCB in the same procedure demonstrated poor agreement between the diagnosis derived from TBCB findings and the diagnosis derived from SLB findings when the biopsy specimens were reviewed in a blinded manner and without MDD (55). However, more recent larger studies have demonstrated support for the use of TBCB as an alternative to SLB in patients.

FOCUSED REVIEW

3. If a confident clinical diagnosis cannot be made and SLB results are likely to alter treatment decisions, then is the patient at acceptable risk for complications of SLB?

# Minimizing the Risks of SLB

SLB can typically be obtained via VATS approaches. Overall, the data indicate that the use of VATS results in lower morbidity and mortality than the use of open lung biopsy, with a lower rate of pleural effusions, PTX, persistent air leak, and hemothorax being shown. VATS also typically has shorter operative times, reduced analgesic use, and shorter pleural drainage durations and hospital stays (63–65). In both open lung biopsy and VATS, anesthesia management can be modified to attempt to reduce the risk of ventilator-associated lung injury (believed to be a cause of acute exacerbations), including minimizing the fraction of inspired oxygen, tidal volume, and peak airway pressure, as well as the amount of perioperative fluid. One potential method of avoiding ventilator-associated lung injury altogether is to perform nonintubated SLB via VATS techniques, which has been shown to be safe and feasible (66). Although no randomized data comparing intubated with nonintubated SLB are available, some retrospective studies suggest that there are fewer complications with nonintubated approaches (66–68).

Consideration of each of the above questions in MDD, or referral to a specialty center for MDD, may aid the clinician in the decision of whether or not to recommend undergoing SLB to an individual patient. Once the clinician has made the decision to recommend SLB, it is important to recognize that decision thresholds are relatively arbitrary and value-based and that individual patients may value benefits and risks differently from clinicians. Therefore, we suggest that patients should be engaged in an informed decision-making discussion about the expected benefits (questions 1 and 2) and risks of SLB (question 3), which should be weighed against the alternative decision of initiating empiric therapy or expectant management on the basis of the provisional or leading diagnosis or diagnoses.

# Minimizing the Risks and Maximizing the Yield of SLB

Once the decision has been made to proceed to SLB, it is important to implement strategies to reduce the risk of postoperative complications and maximize the procedure’s diagnostic yield. Communication among involved practitioners and institutional protocols implementing these strategies should be considered.

# Maximizing the Yield of SLB

The yield of a SLB depends on optimal sampling to avoid diagnostic bias due to undersampling of the lung tissue and/or disease regions. Considerations for optimal tissue sampling by SLB are summarized in Table 3. Multiple biopsies are needed to assess disease distribution and ensure sufficient sampling for a heterogeneous disease (1, 2, 69). At least one biopsy from two different lobes should be obtained (1, 2, 69). The biopsy location should be correlated with preoperative HRCT scan results to identify areas of high yield for abnormalities and also to avoid areas that only show dense fibrosis and honeycomb changes (1, 2, 70). The tips of the lingula or the right middle lobe of the lung should generally be avoided, as they might show edge artifacts mimicking honeycomb changes (71). Consideration should be given to choosing areas at the interface between fibrotic areas and the preserved lung parenchyma. Sampling error should always be considered, especially if the disease is central. Palpation of the tissue and clamps should be avoided to minimize hemorrhaging and atelectasis. If the disease is unevenly distributed between the right and left lung at evaluation by HRCT, this should be considered when choosing which side to biopsy. If the disease is symmetrical, no data suggest that one side of the lung is safer to biopsy than the other (69).

One study suggested that the right side should be preferred because of the availability of three lobes for tissue sampling (72). Requirements for the size of biopsy specimens are not well studied. On the basis of historical pathologic practice, a biopsy specimen size of 3 x 2 x 1 cm (uninflated) should be sufficient (2). There is no evidence that open SLB or SLB by VATS is superior regarding the yields required for diagnostic accuracy (63, 64, 73–75).

| Surgical sampling                                                                                            | Pathologic assessment                                                                                                          |
| ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Multiple biopsy samples from two to three lobes (minimum of one biopsy sample from two different lobes each) | Confirm presence of diagnostic material at frozen section laboratory if possible                                               |
| Correlate biopsy location with prior HRCT findings                                                           | Fix tissue in formalin for at least 1 h with gentle agitation or formalin injection before sectioning                          |
| Avoid sampling of tips of lingula and right middle lobe                                                      | Cut specimen with sharp knife without squeezing and pushing                                                                    |
| Sampling of interface between the area of fibrosis and the architecturally preserved lung parenchyma         | H\&E: most important stain; possibly add stains for microorganisms, elastic stain, and/or iron on the basis of H\&E impression |
| Biopsy sample size of approximately 3 x 2 x 1 cm (uninflated)                                                | Possibly seek expert opinion                                                                                                   |
| Do not feel or squeeze specimen and avoid clamps in operating room                                           | Multidisciplinary discussion                                                                                                   |

Definition of abbreviations: H&E = hematoxylin and eosin; HRCT = high-resolution computed tomography. Data are from References 1, 2, 63, 64, 69, and 71–75.

pages.md:
# FOCUSED REVIEW

Intraoperative frozen section evaluation of surgical lung biopsy, which is very important to the ILD, and particularly the IPF, patient community. If this information had been available in 2015, I may have avoided the SLB. My HRCTs, lung function evaluations for FVC [forced vital capacity] and DLCO [diffusing capacity of the lung for carbon monoxide], and regular clinician visits may have confirmed my diagnosis of IPF with a high degree of certainty. This document will be a great resource for pulmonologists and their patients.

The specimen may be performed to evaluate the adequacy of tissue sampling. In the frozen section laboratory, a single thin piece of tissue is carefully cut from the specimen by using a sharp knife, without pushing or squeezing the specimen. If no abnormal tissue is present or only end-stage fibrotic lung tissue is identified, additional biopsy sampling may be recommended. The final diagnosis should be established on the permanent section slides.

For permanent section evaluation, SLB tissues must be adequately inflated to prevent atelectasis that may hinder pathologic assessment. This can be achieved either by carefully injecting the tissue with formalin (using a small-bore needle and syringe) or by floating the specimen in formalin with repeated gentle shaking of the container for a minimum of an hour. The entirety of the SLB specimens should be submitted for microscopic assessment. Hematoxylin and eosin staining is the most important stain for microscopic evaluation. Occasionally, a trichrome-stained sample may be evaluated to further assess the fibrotic component. Special stains for microorganisms (Grocott’s methenamine silver stain, periodic acid–Schiff stain, acid-fast bacteria stain, or equivalent) should be performed if there is clinical concern for infection or if there are histologic features concerning for or suggestive of potential infection. An elastic stain may be used to assess vascular changes, and an iron stain may be used if there is concern about asbestos-related lung disease.

We suggest that the interpretation of SLB results be performed by a pathologist with expertise in ILD. The correlation with HRCT and clinical information is also important for pathologists to consider when interpreting SLB results.

# Conclusions

Diagnosis of ILD requires a MDD approach that involves clinicians, radiologists, and pathologists (1). Although pathology plays a key role in the diagnosis of ILD, the increased confidence and accuracy of the diagnosis gained from microscopic pathologic assessment of SLB specimens must be balanced with the associated risks in patients with ILD.

Mr. Rick Rudell, a patient with IPF and a Pulmonary Fibrosis Foundation Ambassador and Support Group Leader, was invited to review this document. Mr. Rudell underwent an SLB in 2016 and experienced a subsequent acute exacerbation that resulted in a prolonged intensive care unit course. “This is a remarkable document on the subject of.”

Author disclosures are available with the text of this article at www.atsjournals.org.
